article_title: Right to be heard and access to the file
article_text:
Article 30 — Right to be heard and access to the file
1. Before adopting a decision pursuant to Article 7, Article 8(1), Article 9(1), Articles 15, 16, 22, 23, 25 and 26 and Article 27(2), the Commission shall give the gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned the opportunity of being heard on:
(a)preliminary findings of the Commission, including any matter to which the Commission has taken objections;
(b)measures that the Commission may intend to take in view of the preliminary findings pursuant to point (a) of this paragraph.

2. Gatekeepers, undertakings and associations of undertakings concerned may submit their observations to the Commission’s preliminary findings within a time limit which shall be fixed by the Commission in its preliminary findings and which may not be less than 14 days.

3. The Commission shall base its decisions only on objections on which gatekeepers, undertakings and associations of undertakings concerned have been able to comment.

4. The rights of defence of the gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned shall be fully respected in any proceedings. The gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned shall be entitled to have access to the Commission's file under the terms of a negotiated disclosure, subject to the legitimate interest of undertakings in the protection of their business secrets. The right of access to the file shall not extend to confidential information and internal documents of the Commission or the authorities of the Member States. In particular, the right of access shall not extend to correspondence between the Commission and the authorities of the Member States. Nothing in this paragraph shall prevent the Commission from disclosing and using information necessary to prove an infringement.
provision_effect: Establishes the right of gatekeepers and undertakings to be heard before Commission decisions, to submit observations within a minimum 14-day period, and to access the Commission's file, with full respect for rights of defence.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Amadeus explicitly advocates that "companies' rights of defense during Commission proceedings should be respected," which directly corresponds to Article 30's concrete establishment of rights of defence, the right to be heard, and access to the file. The provision takes specific action in exactly the direction Amadeus advocated for.

organisation: UNI Europa - The European Services Workers Union
source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3430829 - 30/06/2020 Response to Inception Impact Assessment (IIA): Digital Services Act package: Ex ante regulatory instrument for large online platforms with significant network effects acting as gatekeepers in the European Union’s internal market There is an urgent need to create fair competitive conditions in the platform industry. Recognise the worker dimension Throughout the Digital Services Act (DSA) documents there is mention of consumers, sellers and intermediaries, but no mention of platform workers, while workers are just as much a part of this equation. As the DSA address the obligations and responsibilities of platforms, it is therefore necessary to recognise the employer responsibilities and employment relationship as well. As indicated in the Roadmap, ‘ways of improving the labour conditions of platform workers’ is treated as a separate measure. Especially as the ‘look at ways…’ formulated offers no promise of any initiative, we find it necessary to emphasise that it is imperative that the improvement of the labour conditions of platform workers is recognised at equal footing as falling within the realm of obligations and responsibilities of platforms as employers. In short, the DSA and the measure to improve working conditions for platform workers must be interlinked and mutually reinforced
context_after: In short, the DSA and the measure to improve working conditions for platform workers must be interlinked and mutually reinforced. The important problem to solve in relation to platform workers is the misclassification of workers as self-employed, which in some countries can limit their ability to engage in collective bargaining. To rectify this, it is necessary to recognise the employment relationship between platform and platform worker. Platform obligations The DSA should establish clear obligations and prohibited practices for these large online platforms with economic power, application of which may provide European consumers and business users more choice and access to innovative solutions. It should establish clear obligations that these platforms would be required to comply with, including allowing workers to make use of their right to collective bargaining (e.g. through an ex ante regulatory tool that complements the P2B Regulation). Platforms should not be allowed to be both a marketplace and a market participant. We welcome the recent reports that the EC will bring antitrust charges against Amazon on this issue. During the Covid-19 crisis, Amazon demonstrated its unfair market power repeatedly by making unilateral decisions that impacted thousands of business around the world.i UNI europa – European Services Workers Union Rue Joseph II, 40 | 1000 Brussels | Belgium | Tel: +32 2 234 5656 | Fax: +32 2 235 0870 | www.uni-europa.org 1/2
article_text:
Article 38 — Review
1. By DD/MM/YYYY, and subsequently every three years, the Commission shall evaluate this Regulation and report to the European Parliament, the Council and the European Economic and Social Committee.

2. The evaluations shall establish whether additional rules, including regarding the list of core platform services laid down in point 2 of Article 2, the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 and their enforcement, may be required to ensure that digital markets across the Union are contestable and fair. Following the evaluations, the Commission shall take appropriate measures, which may include legislative proposals.

3. Member States shall provide any relevant information they have that the Commission may require for the purposes of drawing up the report referred to in paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a periodic review mechanism requiring the Commission to evaluate the Regulation every three years and consider whether additional rules or legislative proposals are needed to ensure contestable and fair digital markets.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's position specifically advocates for recognising platform workers' rights and employer responsibilities within the DSA framework, with no substantive stance on the review/evaluation mechanism itself. The provision establishes a periodic review process focused on market contestability and fairness, which does not engage with the worker dimension UNI Europa advocates for.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3431555 - 30/06/2020 EPC Recommendations on Inception Impact Assessment for the Digital Services Act Package: ex ante regulatory instrument of very large online platforms acting as gatekeepers, 30 June 2010 (Transparency Register no: 4456380381-30) The European Publishers Council (EPC) is a high-level group of Chairmen and CEOs of Europe’s leading media groups representing companies which are active in news media, television, radio, digital marketplaces, journals, e-Learning, databases and books. We have been communicating with Europe’s legislators since 1991 on issues that affect the health and viability of journalistically driven media and publishing companies which uphold the freedom of expression, media diversity, and democratic debate in the European Union. This contribution is an extract from our full Policy Vision Paper which presents the EPC’s proposals to deliver an appropriate framework for a sustainable and independent media sector to thrive in Europe in the digital era.   The EPC considers that although the options presented are not mutually exclusive, a combination of options under 3 (3.a and 3.b) from the Road Map would provide the best approach to, at the same time, include a clear list of prohibited practices and a tailor-made set of remedies to be applied on a case-by-case basis
context_after: With this in mind the EPC asks the European Commission to establish an ex ante regulatory regime specifically designed to tackle the challenges posed by market dominant platforms. This has to be tackled as a matter of urgency given the speed of change in the market. This should include: (i) Full transparency in the way in which the dominant platforms conduct business: in particular how they collect or use data. (ii) An obligation to provide audited figures on the reach on their platforms, or on the revenues derived from publishers’ users. (iii) Non-discriminatory access for legal publications and offerings to market dominant digital platforms including a prohibition of preferential treatment of own products and
article_text:
Article 32 — Digital Markets Advisory Committee
1. The Commission shall be assisted by the Digital Markets Advisory Committee. That Committee shall be a Committee within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.

2. Where the opinion of the committee is to be obtained by written procedure, that procedure shall be terminated without result when, within the time-limit for delivery of the opinion, the chair of the committee so decides or a simple majority of committee members so request.

3. The Commission shall communicate the opinion of the Digital Markets Advisory Committee to the addressee of an individual decision, together with that decision. It shall make the opinion public together with the individual decision, having regard to the legitimate interest in the protection of professional secrecy.

4. Where reference is made to this paragraph, Article 4 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 shall apply.
provision_effect: Establishes the Digital Markets Advisory Committee to assist the Commission, defining its procedural rules including written procedures, communication of opinions, and applicable regulation.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The EPC's position focuses on advocating for a combination of prohibited practices and tailor-made remedies under the ex ante regulatory framework, with no mention of or stance on an advisory committee structure. The org text contains no substantive advocacy position related to the establishment or functioning of the Digital Markets Advisory Committee.

source_text: We see the merits of these arguments and would therefore support having only a broadly applicable NCT as it is outlined by the Commission in Option 3 of the inception impact assessment. This does not mean that we fully oppose a gatekeeper regulation. We recognize that there is a strong political preference for having such a separate instrument. However, it means a possible gatekeeper regulation must be constructed in a very narrow way to avoid any unnecessary overlap. It should focus on enforcement efficiencies by addressing recurring business practices where there is a very strong presumption that they are harming competition.8 A targeted version of Option 3a of the Commission’s inception impact assessment could be in line with such an objective. Below we discuss in further detail the criteria that a broadly constructed NCT and a targeted gatekeeper regulation would have to fulfill to be truly complementary to competition law and complementing each other. Procedurally, as the Commission has stated itself, all remedies under the NCT and/or the gatekeeper regulation need to apply ex nunc and no fines should be retroactively imposed for past conduct. Of course, this leaves open the possibility of a regular infringement procedure for breach of EU competition law for past anti-competitive behaviour. III
context_before: Additionally, it increases legal certainty and predictability.” 5
context_after: Of course, this leaves open the possibility of a regular infringement procedure for breach of EU competition law for past anti-competitive behaviour. III. New Competition Tool Non-sectoral ex-ante regulation needs to be flexible enough to be applicable in an effective manner across all sectors, each with its own set of technologies, business models, demand patterns, and competitive dynamics. This necessitates a case-by-case approach, assessing thoroughly each market before taking or recommending regulatory action. We believe that the NCT, which would extend the Commission’s current market inquiry powers to impose remedies on companies to ensure or restore effective competition absent a finding of abuse, is in line with such an approach. We welcome that the NCT requires a market inquiry before any remedies can be proposed. Structural impediments to competition can best be assessed per market and are hard to infer from the presence or conduct of a single firm. Markets are “relevant to identify barriers to entry, which, in turn, are important to assess the market power of incumbent firms,” as Peitz and Franck rightly point out. One needs to know what the competitive restraints for firms in a given market are and whether new competition is likely to emerge when prices are too high or when there is problematic conduct. As a “competition tool”, the NCT should stay as close as possible to established concepts in EU competition law
article_text:
Article 16 — Market investigation into systematic non-compliance
1. Where the market investigation shows that a gatekeeper has systematically infringed the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 and has further strengthened or extended its gatekeeper position in relation to the characteristics under Article 3(1), the Commission may by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) impose on such gatekeeper any behavioural or structural remedies which are proportionate to the infringement committed and necessary to ensure compliance with this Regulation. The Commission shall conclude its investigation by adopting a decision within twelve months from the opening of the market investigation.

2. The Commission may only impose structural remedies pursuant to paragraph 1 either where there is no equally effective behavioural remedy or where any equally effective behavioural remedy would be more burdensome for the gatekeeper concerned than the structural remedy.

3. A gatekeeper shall be deemed to have engaged in a systematic non-compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6, where the Commission has issued at least three non-compliance or fining decisions pursuant to Articles 25 and 26 respectively against a gatekeeper in relation to any of its core platform services within a period of five years prior to the adoption of the decision opening a market investigation in view of the possible adoption of a decision pursuant to this Article.

4. A gatekeeper shall be deemed to have further strengthened or extended its gatekeeper position in relation to the characteristics under Article 3(1), where its impact on the internal market has further increased, its importance as a gateway for business users to reach end users has further increased or the gatekeeper enjoys a further entrenched and durable position in its operations.

5. The Commission shall communicate its objections to the gatekeeper concerned within six months from the opening of the investigation. In its objections, the Commission shall explain whether it preliminarily considers that the conditions of paragraph 1 are met and which remedy or remedies it preliminarily considers necessary and proportionate.

6. The Commission may at any time during the market investigation extend its duration where the extension is justified on objective grounds and proportionate. The extension may apply to the deadline by which the Commission has to issue its objections, or to the deadline for adoption of the final decision. The total duration of any extension or extensions pursuant to this paragraph shall not exceed six months.The Commission may consider commitments pursuant to Article 23 and make them binding in its decision.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to impose behavioural or structural remedies on gatekeepers found to have systematically infringed obligations under Articles 5 and 6, defined as at least three non-compliance or fining decisions within five years.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Booking.com advocates for a narrowly constructed gatekeeper regulation focused on enforcement efficiencies and recurring harmful practices, and explicitly states that remedies should apply ex nunc with no retroactive fines. Article 16 establishes a systematic non-compliance mechanism with structural and behavioural remedies, which partially aligns with the org's acceptance of a targeted gatekeeper regulation, but it is genuinely unclear whether the provision's specific threshold (three decisions in five years) and remedy scope satisfy or contradict Booking.com's call for a "very narrow" and targeted instrument — the connection is real but the direction of alignment is ambiguous.

article_text:
Article 25 — Non-compliance
1. The Commission shall adopt a non-compliance decision in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) where it finds that a gatekeeper does not comply with one or more of the following:
(a)any of the obligations laid down in Articles 5 or 6;
(b)measures specified in a decision adopted pursuant to Article 7(2);
(c)measures ordered pursuant to Article 16(1);
(d)interim measures ordered pursuant to Article 22; or
(e)commitments made legally binding pursuant to Article 23.

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the gatekeeper concerned. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain the measures it considers to take or it considers that the gatekeeper should take in order to effectively address the preliminary findings.

3. In the non-compliance decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall order the gatekeeper to cease and desist with the non-compliance within an appropriate deadline and to provide explanations on how it plans to comply with the decision.

4. The gatekeeper shall provide the Commission with the description of the measures it took to ensure compliance with the decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1.

5. Where the Commission finds that the conditions of paragraph 1 are not met, it shall close the investigation by a decision.
provision_effect: Establishes the procedure for the Commission to adopt non-compliance decisions against gatekeepers who fail to meet obligations, including ordering them to cease and desist and provide compliance plans.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Booking.com advocates for a narrowly constructed gatekeeper regulation focused on enforcement efficiencies, with remedies applying ex nunc and no retroactive fines. Article 25 establishes the non-compliance decision mechanism but does not explicitly address retroactivity of fines or the scope of the regulation's obligations — those are governed elsewhere. There is a real connection (the org's procedural concerns about enforcement relate to this enforcement mechanism), but it is genuinely unclear whether this provision satisfies, contradicts, or ignores the org's specific asks about ex nunc application and no retroactive fines.

source_text: ﹣ strict proportionality to remedy any perceived issue being corrected The NCT should be applicable across all sectors subject to some substantive limitations. In particular, the use of the NCT needs to be limited to instances of structural risks to competition and structural lack of competition, and the imposition of remedies should only be permissible where competition law enforcement is insufficient (and in markets with structural risk to competition also taking into consideration alternatives such as interim measures). The Commission should have the burden of proof to show that at least one of these conditions is satisfied before remedies could be imposed on one or more market participants. This burden should be quite substantial as not to create any incentives to substitute ex-ante for ex-post enforcement as a procedural shortcut. As the remedies to be imposed by the Commission are meant to address the specific shortcomings in a market and markets vary substantially, there are not many limits to the Commission’s possible actions. This holds the Commission to a special responsibility as these potentially far reaching remedies will be applied without any finding of wrongful conduct. It also means that there needs to be an opportunity for swift recourse to judicial review to challenge the Commission’s action. We believe it is worth revisiting the debate on whether or not the EU needs a specialized competition court
context_before: As a “competition tool”, the NCT should stay as close as possible to established concepts in EU competition law. In particular, the following standards should be preserved: ﹣ consumer welfare standard as the objective of EU competition policy ﹣ effects based approach to assess any potentially pro- or anti-competitive conduct ﹣ requirement and methodology to define the relevant market ﹣ evidentiary standards in assessing the effectiveness of competition ﹣ procedural rights, e.g. the right to be heard and challenge preliminary findings, access to evidence 8 This would be in line with the Commission objective of curbing “large-scale unfair trading practices”. 6
context_after: We believe it is worth revisiting the debate on whether or not the EU needs a specialized competition court. Such a court would be able to expedite the judicial review as it would be staffed with competition experts and it could also scrutinize the Commission’s decisions in a more substantial way. In this context, it is worth exploring if the standard of judicial review should change for decisions taken under the NCT. The current standard of “manifest error” might grant the Commission too much discretion when it is applied to matters where there was no full investigation and no finding of wrongful conduct. We note that the market investigation powers held by the CMA are kept in check by the Competition Appeals Tribunal which specialises in competition cases to allow effective, timely and appropriate scrutiny. Substantively, the Commission is right to focus on those markets that are hard to contest, e.g. with strong economies of scale and network effects, lack of multi-homing, customer lock in and high switching costs. However, we want to stress that it is important to thoroughly assess the competitive dynamics and pace of technological change of each market. As we illustrate in the next section below by example of the accommodation market, the presence of the above mentioned market characteristics alone is insufficient to draw any conclusions about competitive dynamics. Rather they need to be assessed in context
article_text:
Article 19 — Requests for information
1. The Commission may by simple request or by decision require information from undertakings and associations of undertakings to provide all necessary information, including for the purpose of monitoring, implementing and enforcing the rules laid down in this Regulation. The Commission may also request access to data bases and algorithms of undertakings and request explanations on those by a simple request or by a decision.

2. The Commission may request information from undertakings and associations of undertakings pursuant to paragraph 1 also prior to opening a market investigation pursuant to Article 14 or proceedings pursuant to Article 18.

3. When sending a simple request for information to an undertaking or association of undertakings, the Commission shall state the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and fix the time-limit within which the information is to be provided, and the penalties provided for in Article 26 for supplying incomplete, incorrect or misleading information or explanations.

4. Where the Commission requires undertakings and associations of undertakings to supply information by decision, it shall state the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and fix the time-limit within which it is to be provided. Where the Commission requires undertakings to provide access to its data-bases and algorithms, it shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, and fix the time-limit within which it is to be provided. It shall also indicate the penalties provided for in Article 26 and indicate or impose the periodic penalty payments provided for in Article 27. It shall further indicate the right to have the decision reviewed by the Court of Justice.

5. The undertakings or associations of undertakings or their representatives shall supply the information requested on behalf of the undertaking or the association of undertakings concerned. Lawyers duly authorised to act may supply the information on behalf of their clients. The latter shall remain fully responsible if the information supplied is incomplete, incorrect or misleading.

6. At the request of the Commission, the governments and authorities of the Member States shall provide the Commission with all necessary information to carry out the duties assigned to it by this Regulation.
provision_effect: Establishes the Commission's power to request information, access to databases and algorithms from undertakings by simple request or decision, including prior to opening formal investigations.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's position focuses on proportionality of remedies, burden of proof, limits on the New Competition Tool (NCT), and judicial review of Commission decisions — not on information-gathering powers. The provision deals specifically with the Commission's investigative information request powers, which is a distinct subject not addressed in the org's submission. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to this provision's subject matter.

source_text: It should only be applicable to platforms that are gatekeepers and will remain so for the foreseeable future, i.e. platforms that fulfill certain criteria and in particular operate in markets that are not contestable, and not all platforms. Under the current system the EU Commission and national competition authorities share a joint responsibility for enforcing EU competition rules. However, in line with the nature of platforms to be regulated, namely those that hold market power over a significant part of the EU’s internal market, we believe that DG Competition should be the sole authority to designate platforms to be gatekeepers and impose behavioral obligations. This is also necessary to ensure consistency with the parallel application of EU competition law and the NCT. Below we provide further detail on the criteria to define gatekeeper platforms and the conduct to be regulated. Traditionally, a gatekeeper has been seen in competition economics as a “ strategic bottleneck”, a dominant firm that guards access to one side of its platform which primarily single homes. However, this criteria alone is insufficient because there is no justification for why these features should give rise to such grave competition concerns that justify regulation in addition to competition law enforcement and the NCT

article_text:
Article 15 — Market investigation for designating gatekeepers
1. The Commission may conduct a market investigation for the purpose of examining whether a provider of core platform services should be designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(6), or in order to identify core platform services for a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(7). It shall endeavour to conclude its investigation by adopting a decision in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) within twelve months from the opening of the market investigation.

2. In the course of a market investigation pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall endeavour to communicate its preliminary findings to the provider of core platform services concerned within six months from the opening of the investigation. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain whether it considers, on a provisional basis, that the provider of core platform services should be designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(6).

3. Where the provider of core platform services satisfies the thresholds set out in Article 3(2), but has presented significantly substantiated arguments in accordance with Article 3(4), the Commission shall endeavour to conclude the market investigation within five months from the opening of the market investigation by a decision pursuant to paragraph 1. In that case the Commission shall endeavour to communicate its preliminary findings pursuant to paragraph 2 to the provider of core platform services within three months from the opening of the investigation.

4. When the Commission pursuant to Article 3(6) designates as a gatekeeper a provider of core platform services that does not yet enjoy an entrenched and durable position in its operations, but it is foreseeable that it will enjoy such a position in the near future, it shall declare applicable to that gatekeeper only obligations laid down in Article 5(b) and Article 6(1) points (e), (f), (h) and (i) as specified in the designation decision. The Commission shall only declare applicable those obligations that are appropriate and necessary to prevent that the gatekeeper concerned achieves by unfair means an entrenched and durable position in its operations. The Commission shall review such a designation in accordance with the procedure laid down in Article 4.
provision_effect: Establishes a market investigation procedure for the Commission to designate providers of core platform services as gatekeepers, including timelines and conditions for applying obligations to providers not yet entrenched.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Booking.com advocates that gatekeeper designation should be limited to platforms that will remain gatekeepers for the foreseeable future and that DG Competition should be the sole designating authority. The provision establishes a Commission-led market investigation procedure for gatekeeper designation, which partially aligns with the idea of a centralised Commission role, but also includes provisions for designating platforms not yet entrenched (Article 15(4)), which runs counter to Booking.com's position that only platforms that "will remain" gatekeepers should be covered. The connection is real but the direction is genuinely ambiguous — the provision both partially satisfies and partially contradicts the org's stated position.

article_text:
Article 10 — Updating obligations for gatekeepers
1. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 34 to update the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 where, based on a market investigation pursuant to Article 17, it has identified the need for new obligations addressing practices that limit the contestability of core platform services or are unfair in the same way as the practices addressed by the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6.

1. A practice within the meaning of paragraph 1 shall be considered to be unfair or limit the contestability of core platform services where:
(a)there is an imbalance of rights and obligations on business users and the gatekeeper is obtaining an advantage from business users that is disproportionate to the service provided by the gatekeeper to business users; or
(b)the contestability of markets is weakened as a consequence of such a practice engaged in by gatekeepers.
provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts to update gatekeeper obligations under Articles 5 and 6 based on market investigations identifying new unfair or contestability-limiting practices.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Booking.com advocates for a narrow gatekeeper designation limited to platforms in non-contestable markets, with DG Competition as the sole designating authority, and raises concerns about the criteria used to define gatekeepers and the conduct to be regulated. Article 10 establishes a mechanism for the Commission to expand gatekeeper obligations via delegated acts based on market investigations — touching on contestability and unfairness criteria that Booking.com discusses. However, while Booking.com's position is broadly skeptical of over-broad regulation and favors stricter criteria, it is genuinely unclear whether this specific updating mechanism satisfies, contradicts, or ignores their advocacy: the provision does reference contestability as a criterion (which aligns with Booking.com's framing), but the broader enforcement architecture (Commission-led, not DG Competition-sole authority) and the expansive delegated power could equally be seen as contrary to their position.

article_text:
Article 12 — Obligation to inform about concentrations
1. A gatekeeper shall inform the Commission of any intended concentration within the meaning of Article 3 of Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 involving another provider of core platform services or of any other services provided in the digital sector irrespective of whether it is notifiable to a Union competition authority under Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 or to a competent national competition authority under national merger rules.
A gatekeeper shall inform the Commission of such a concentration prior to its implementation and following the conclusion of the agreement, the announcement of the public bid, or the acquisition of a controlling interest.

2. The notification pursuant to paragraph 1 shall at least describe for the acquisition targets their EEA and worldwide annual turnover, for any relevant core platform services their respective EEA annual turnover, their number of yearly active business users and the number of monthly active end users, as well as the rationale of the intended concentration.

3. If, following any concentration as provided in paragraph 1, additional core platform services individually satisfy the thresholds in point (b) of Article 3(2), the gatekeeper concerned shall inform the Commission thereof within three months from the implementation of the concentration and provide the Commission with the information referred to in Article 3(2).
provision_effect: Requires gatekeepers to notify the Commission of any intended concentration involving another provider of core platform services or digital sector services, regardless of whether it meets merger notification thresholds.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The org's position focuses on the criteria for designating gatekeepers and which authority (DG Competition vs. NCAs) should have jurisdiction over gatekeeper designation and obligations — it does not express any specific stance on merger/concentration notification obligations for gatekeepers. There is no substantive advocacy position in the org text that relates to the concentration reporting mechanism established in Article 12.

context_after: The terms of reference for the Commission’s support study and academic literature (e.g. Franck/Peitz: Market definition and market power in the platform economy)​ have laid out quantitative and qualitative criteria that could be employed to identify such market power. We believe the following indicative criteria could be useful for such an analysis: ﹣ market significance (e.g. user shares relative to potential market size/market share of transactions/time spent on platform/unique users/unique visitors – depending of type of platform) ﹣ third parties’ turn-over realised on a platform ﹣ strength of network effects ﹣ degree of single homing ﹣ lock-in ﹣ switching costs These criteria are not cumulative and it will take the judgement of the relevant authority to employ the right criteria in the right circumstances. For example, for a social media platform it might be adequate to look at time spent on the platform relative to other attention markets to approximate its significance. For a marketplace, it would be more appropriate to look at its shares of overall transactions in the market to identify its significance. Online and offline constraints must be considered carefully as part of this assessment, in particular as the distinction between on- and offline becomes increasingly meaningless in many markets. The third condition reflects the conglomerate nature of digital gatekeepers

article_text:
Article 8 — Suspension
1. The Commission may, on a reasoned request by the gatekeeper, exceptionally suspend, in whole or in part, a specific obligation laid down in Articles 5 and 6 for a core platform service by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), where the gatekeeper demonstrates that compliance with that specific obligation would endanger, due to exceptional circumstances beyond the control of the gatekeeper, the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union, and only to the extent necessary to address such threat to its viability. The Commission shall aim to adopt the suspension decision without delay and at the latest 3 months following receipt of a complete reasoned request.

2. Where the suspension is granted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall review its suspension decision every year. Following such a review the Commission shall either lift the suspension or decide that the conditions of paragraph 1 continue to be met.

3. The Commission may, acting on a reasoned request by a gatekeeper, provisionally suspend the application of the relevant obligation to one or more individual core platform services already prior to the decision pursuant to paragraph 1.
In assessing the request, the Commission shall take into account, in particular, the impact of the compliance with the specific obligation on the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union as well as on third parties. The suspension may be made subject to conditions and obligations to be defined by the Commission in order to ensure a fair balance between these interests and the objectives of this Regulation. Such a request may be made and granted at any time pending the assessment of the Commission pursuant to paragraph 1.

article_text:
Article 11 — Anti-circumvention
1. A gatekeeper shall ensure that the obligations of Articles 5 and 6 are fully and effectively complied with. While the obligations of Articles 5 and 6 apply in respect of core platform services designated pursuant to Article 3, their implementation shall not be undermined by any behaviour of the undertaking to which the gatekeeper belongs, regardless of whether this behaviour is of a contractual, commercial, technical or any other nature.

2. Where consent for collecting and processing of personal data is required to ensure compliance with this Regulation, a gatekeeper shall take the necessary steps to either enable business users to directly obtain the required consent to their processing, where required under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and Directive 2002/58/EC, or to comply with Union data protection and privacy rules and principles in other ways including by providing business users with duly anonymised data where appropriate. The gatekeeper shall not make the obtaining of this consent by the business user more burdensome than for its own services.

3. A gatekeeper shall not degrade the conditions or quality of any of the core platform services provided to business users or end users who avail themselves of the rights or choices laid down in Articles 5 and 6, or make the exercise of those rights or choices unduly difficult.
provision_effect: Requires gatekeepers to ensure full and effective compliance with Articles 5 and 6 obligations, prohibiting circumvention through any contractual, commercial, or technical behaviour, and preventing degradation of services for users exercising their rights.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text focuses exclusively on advocating for specific cumulative criteria to define which platforms qualify as gatekeepers (market size, user dependency, and ability to expand market power). It does not express any position on anti-circumvention mechanisms, compliance obligations, or the degradation of services — the subjects of Article 11. The org's position is therefore unrelated to what this provision establishes.

source_text: It thus fits the criteria of recurring anti-competitive business practice. However, it is important to stress that self-preferencing is not per se an abusive practice, even by a gatekeeper platform. There always needs to be a careful analysis to determine whether the facts of the particular case warrant the presumption of harmful conduct. For example, a vertically integrated platform might give preference to its own lower priced products vis-a-vis downstream competitors. Such a conduct might well be consumer welfare enhancing, even if it is to the detriment of competitors. Further, in line with the German proposal, we believe that it should be a two-step process. First, the Commission would designate a platform to be a gatekeeper. Second, it would determine which prohibitions from the enumerated list above apply to the gatekeeper. This means they would not apply automatically and not all of them have to be imposed. This is owed to the fact that platforms differ vastly and not all of the conducts on the list might be applicable. There should also be the option for a gatekeeper platform to voluntarily notify business practices that are in conflict with the enumerated list to get clearance from the EU Commission. Prohibitions levied by the EU Commission should be time limited and the Commission should be under an obligation, on application of the platform in question, to re-assess them if market conditions have changed

The substance of ex-ante regulation should build upon the existing body of economic knowledge and established theories of harm. The principle should be that gatekeeper platforms face certain conduct limitations, according to the procedure outlined below, unless they can claim objective justification or efficiencies for deviating from them. Such a limited reversal of the burden of proof is in line with other recent proposals, e.g. the German competition law draft, and recommendations of various expert reports, e.g. the EU expert report. It is important that only conduct where there is a strong presumption that it is anti-competitive when employed by a gatekeeper platform should be restricted. In our opinion, the main focus should be on self-preferencing of vertically integrated platforms. Gatekeeper platforms, in particular vertically integrated non-transaction platforms, can have an incentive to leverage their market power unfairly from one market to another to exclude rivals, e.g. by giving preferential treatment to one’s own products and services. Self-preferencing is at the heart of a majority of the Commission’s cases involving digital platforms. It thus fits the criteria of recurring anti-competitive business practice. However, it is important to stress that self-preferencing is not per se an abusive practice, even by a gatekeeper platform

article_title: Review of the status of gatekeepers
article_text:
Article 4 — Review of the status of gatekeepers
1. The Commission may upon request or its own initiative reconsider, amend or repeal at any moment a decision adopted pursuant to Article 3 for one of the following reasons:
(a)there has been a substantial change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the undertakings.

2. The Commission shall regularly, and at least every 2 years, review whether the designated gatekeepers continue to satisfy the requirements laid down in Article 3(1), or whether new providers of core platform services satisfy those requirements. The regular review shall also examine whether the list of affected core platform services of the gatekeeper needs to be adjusted.
Where the Commission, on the basis of that review pursuant to the first subparagraph, finds that the facts on which the designation of the providers of core platform services as gatekeepers was based, have changed, it shall adopt a corresponding decision.

3. The Commission shall publish and update the list of gatekeepers and the list of the core platform services for which they need to comply with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 on an on-going basis.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to review, amend, or repeal gatekeeper designations upon request or on its own initiative, and mandates regular reviews at least every two years to assess whether gatekeepers continue to meet designation requirements.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Booking.com explicitly advocates that prohibitions imposed by the Commission should be time-limited and that the Commission should be obligated to re-assess them if market conditions have changed. Article 4 directly responds to this by establishing a formal review mechanism — both on request and periodically (at least every 2 years) — allowing gatekeeper designations and their associated obligations to be reconsidered when facts change, which aligns with the org's specific ask for reassessment obligations tied to changing market conditions.

Where needed, antitrust investigations could be fortified by (i) targeted use of interim measures; (ii) well-designed remedies; (iii) intervention against specific, harmful forms of self-preferencing (complemented by updated guidance); and (iv) use of a new tool along the lines of the proposed NCT. In sum, we believe that there are opportunities for modernisation. The solution to ‘gatekeeper’ concerns is likely to comprise a package of complementary reforms. New regulations could address issues that fall outside the scope of competition law, but which are important for digital markets to function in a fair, efficient manner (e.g., ensuring data portability, appropriate levels of transparency, and fairness in contractual relations). While it is hard to argue for a particular scope of application, it does seem at this initial stage that some of these measures could be important whatever the size or market position of the digital platform at issue. Similarly, as one looks back at the unpredictable nature of historic innovation and tries to imagine what kinds of digital products and services European consumers will be using in the future, it seems reasonable to assume that notions of ‘gatekeeper’, ‘platform’, and even ‘digital’ will need to be robust and flexible. article_title: Market investigation into new services and new practices
article_text:
Article 17 — Market investigation into new services and new practices
1. The Commission may conduct a market investigation with the purpose of examining whether one or more services within the digital sector should be added to the list of core platform services or to detect types of practices that may limit the contestability of core platform services or may be unfair and which are not effectively addressed by this Regulation. It shall issue a public report at the latest within 24 months from the opening of the market investigation.
Where appropriate, that report shall:
(a)be accompanied by a proposal to amend this Regulation in order to include additional services within the digital sector in the list of core platform services laid down in point 2 of Article 2;
(b)be accompanied by a delegated act amending Articles 5 or 6 as provided for in Article 10.
provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to conduct market investigations to add new services to the core platform services list or detect new unfair/anti-contestability practices not covered by the Regulation, with a report due within 24 months.

article_title: Right to be heard and access to the file
article_text:
Article 30 — Right to be heard and access to the file
1. Before adopting a decision pursuant to Article 7, Article 8(1), Article 9(1), Articles 15, 16, 22, 23, 25 and 26 and Article 27(2), the Commission shall give the gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned the opportunity of being heard on:
(a)preliminary findings of the Commission, including any matter to which the Commission has taken objections;
(b)measures that the Commission may intend to take in view of the preliminary findings pursuant to point (a) of this paragraph.

2. Gatekeepers, undertakings and associations of undertakings concerned may submit their observations to the Commission’s preliminary findings within a time limit which shall be fixed by the Commission in its preliminary findings and which may not be less than 14 days.

3. The Commission shall base its decisions only on objections on which gatekeepers, undertakings and associations of undertakings concerned have been able to comment.

4. The rights of defence of the gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned shall be fully respected in any proceedings. The gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned shall be entitled to have access to the Commission's file under the terms of a negotiated disclosure, subject to the legitimate interest of undertakings in the protection of their business secrets. The right of access to the file shall not extend to confidential information and internal documents of the Commission or the authorities of the Member States. In particular, the right of access shall not extend to correspondence between the Commission and the authorities of the Member States. Nothing in this paragraph shall prevent the Commission from disclosing and using information necessary to prove an infringement.
provision_effect: Establishes the right of gatekeepers and undertakings to be heard and access the Commission's file before decisions are adopted, including minimum time limits for submitting observations and full respect for rights of defence.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Google explicitly raised the question of "the extent to which affected undertakings would be given access to file and the right to be heard" as a key design consideration for the new competition tool. Article 30 directly delivers on this by codifying the right to be heard, access to the file, and full respect for rights of defence — precisely the procedural rights Google flagged as warranting consideration. The provision acts in the direction Google advocated for.

article_text:
Article 19 — Requests for information
1. The Commission may by simple request or by decision require information from undertakings and associations of undertakings to provide all necessary information, including for the purpose of monitoring, implementing and enforcing the rules laid down in this Regulation. The Commission may also request access to data bases and algorithms of undertakings and request explanations on those by a simple request or by a decision.

2. The Commission may request information from undertakings and associations of undertakings pursuant to paragraph 1 also prior to opening a market investigation pursuant to Article 14 or proceedings pursuant to Article 18.

3. When sending a simple request for information to an undertaking or association of undertakings, the Commission shall state the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and fix the time-limit within which the information is to be provided, and the penalties provided for in Article 26 for supplying incomplete, incorrect or misleading information or explanations.

4. Where the Commission requires undertakings and associations of undertakings to supply information by decision, it shall state the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and fix the time-limit within which it is to be provided. Where the Commission requires undertakings to provide access to its data-bases and algorithms, it shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, and fix the time-limit within which it is to be provided. It shall also indicate the penalties provided for in Article 26 and indicate or impose the periodic penalty payments provided for in Article 27. It shall further indicate the right to have the decision reviewed by the Court of Justice.

5. The undertakings or associations of undertakings or their representatives shall supply the information requested on behalf of the undertaking or the association of undertakings concerned. Lawyers duly authorised to act may supply the information on behalf of their clients. The latter shall remain fully responsible if the information supplied is incomplete, incorrect or misleading.

6. At the request of the Commission, the governments and authorities of the Member States shall provide the Commission with all necessary information to carry out the duties assigned to it by this Regulation.
provision_effect: Establishes the Commission's power to request information, access to databases and algorithms from undertakings by simple request or decision, including prior to formal investigations, with specified procedural requirements and penalties.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Google's text raises general procedural considerations about the NCT design (deadlines, access to file, rights to be heard, role of courts) without taking a specific directional stance on the Commission's information-gathering powers or access to databases and algorithms. The position is framed as preliminary questions for the Commission to consider rather than a concrete advocacy stance for or against the mechanism established in Article 19.

article_text:
Article 25 — Non-compliance
1. The Commission shall adopt a non-compliance decision in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) where it finds that a gatekeeper does not comply with one or more of the following:
(a)any of the obligations laid down in Articles 5 or 6;
(b)measures specified in a decision adopted pursuant to Article 7(2);
(c)measures ordered pursuant to Article 16(1);
(d)interim measures ordered pursuant to Article 22; or
(e)commitments made legally binding pursuant to Article 23.

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the gatekeeper concerned. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain the measures it considers to take or it considers that the gatekeeper should take in order to effectively address the preliminary findings.

3. In the non-compliance decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall order the gatekeeper to cease and desist with the non-compliance within an appropriate deadline and to provide explanations on how it plans to comply with the decision.

4. The gatekeeper shall provide the Commission with the description of the measures it took to ensure compliance with the decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1.

5. Where the Commission finds that the conditions of paragraph 1 are not met, it shall close the investigation by a decision.
provision_effect: Establishes the procedure for the Commission to adopt a non-compliance decision against a gatekeeper, including preliminary findings, cease-and-desist orders, and closure of investigations.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Google explicitly advocates for procedural safeguards in the NCT regime, including defined review phases, the right to be heard, access to file, and the role of EU courts — all of which relate to due process in enforcement proceedings. Article 25 responds to these concerns by establishing a formal non-compliance procedure that includes communicating preliminary findings to the gatekeeper, giving it the opportunity to explain compliance plans, and requiring a formal decision before action is taken, reflecting the procedural protections Google called for.

context_after: It is premature to draw hard conclusions, with the consultation process having only recently begun. But through a range of measures -- pro-competitive regulation, enhanced antitrust enforcement, and a new competition tool -- a solution that meets this challenge is possible. 10
provision_effect: Establishes implementing acts empowering the Commission to define procedural arrangements for investigations, notifications, deadlines, and rights to be heard under the Digital Markets Act.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Google explicitly advocates for consideration of procedural rights including "the right to be heard," defined review phases, statutory deadlines, and the role of courts — all of which are directly addressed by Article 36's implementing provisions covering practical arrangements for proceedings, extensions of deadlines, and rights to be heard (Articles 30, 16, 15, etc.). The provision concretely establishes the framework for these procedural elements that Google specifically called for the Commission to design carefully.

source_text: In designing the NCT, the Commission should have regard to how it can ensure consistency with that regulatory tradition. In designing the regime, the Commission may wish to consider the following: (1) whether to provide for statutory deadlines, defined review phases, and informal reviews and information gathering prior to a formal investigation; (2) the extent to which affected undertakings would be given access to file and the right to be heard , as well as the extent to which procedural rights would vary between undertakings to whom remedies may apply and other undertakings active in the relevant market(s); and (3) the role of the EU courts, which is particularly important to ensure protection of rights where remedies are imposed, but also whether the Commission would be required to issue an appealable decision following complaints and/or at other preliminary stages. 5. Conclusion The Inception Impact Assessments and consultation documents identify a range of policy measures that aim to address concerns about the operation of digital markets and the Commission’s ability to intervene. With this preliminary response, we have outlined what appears to us the main challenge: addressing regulatory concerns, while preserving innovation and ensuring a positive outcome for European SMEs and consumers. It is premature to draw hard conclusions, with the consultation process having only recently begun

Given that the Commission envisages deploying the NCT in dynamic markets, it may also want to consider how it would readily permit revision or revocation of a remedy where, for example, justified by market developments. The Commission may also want to take into account when considering how to make the regime flexible and proportionate is allowing companies voluntarily to propose remedies, including at different stages of the process (analogous to offering commitments to avoid a reference to Phase 2 in merger control, or the ‘undertakings in lieu’ in the UK market investigation regime). Procedural safeguards. ​The existing European Union competition laws contain robust procedural safeguards to ensure the rights of defence of undertakings concerned and encourage high quality decision making. This practice, supported by the robust judicial review of the European Courts, is an important part of ensuring the Commission’s status as a leading global competition enforcer. article_text:
Article 32 — Digital Markets Advisory Committee
1. The Commission shall be assisted by the Digital Markets Advisory Committee. That Committee shall be a Committee within the meaning of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011.

2. Where the opinion of the committee is to be obtained by written procedure, that procedure shall be terminated without result when, within the time-limit for delivery of the opinion, the chair of the committee so decides or a simple majority of committee members so request.

3. The Commission shall communicate the opinion of the Digital Markets Advisory Committee to the addressee of an individual decision, together with that decision. It shall make the opinion public together with the individual decision, having regard to the legitimate interest in the protection of professional secrecy.

4. Where reference is made to this paragraph, Article 4 of Regulation (EU) No 182/2011 shall apply.
provision_effect: Establishes the Digital Markets Advisory Committee to assist the Commission, defining its procedural rules including written procedure, communication of opinions, and applicable regulatory framework.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Google's position focuses on procedural design elements such as statutory deadlines, access to file, rights to be heard, and the role of EU courts — not on the creation or functioning of an advisory committee. The org text contains no substantive advocacy position on the establishment or operation of a Digital Markets Advisory Committee, making this a subject mismatch rather than a genuine connection.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3431941 - 30/06/2020 Brussels, 30 June 2020 Joint statement in response to the inception impact assessments on a new competition tool ex ante regulatory instrument for large online platforms acting as gatekeepers We are pleased to see that the European Commission is reviewing its regulatory toolbox in response to the challenges posed by large online platforms in numerous digital markets, and we welcome the possibility to provide feedback on both the DG CNECT-led and the DG COMP-led inception impact assessments. The European Commission rightly highlights that a small number of large online platforms are now able to control the entire platform ecosystem, and that they act as economic gatekeepers. The Commission also mentions that these online platforms increasingly determine 'the parameters for future innovations, consumer choice and competition'. We agree, but we are convinced that the challenge is broader than that. We believe that this small number of large online platforms not only act as economic gatekeepers, but also as 'fundamental rights' gatekeepers. Through their business models, their terms of services and community guidelines, these platforms set standards in the market with regards to, among others, consumers' rights to privacy, data protection and freedom of expression
context_after: These large platforms are able to do so because, on the one hand, barriers to entry are so high that it is extremely difficult, if not impossible, for new players to enter the market and put competitive pressure on gatekeepers. And, on the other hand, because consumers do not have viable alternatives to switch to. Data harvesting business models and content curation and distribution practices also result in uncertainty and lack of transparency around how we form our opinion on platforms and how these mechanisms impact, structure, and potentially control the public discourse. In addition to the impact on media pluralism, democratic debate, participation and political processes and fundamental rights, these business choices lead to further engagement on the platforms that helps these companies' that cements their market power. The impact of these platforms' behaviours and business models on the guarantee of fundamental rights in the digital single market is a major challenge for the EU, and the European Commission should include it in its understanding of the problem it aims to fix with these welcomed initiatives.
article_text:
Article 38 — Review
1. By DD/MM/YYYY, and subsequently every three years, the Commission shall evaluate this Regulation and report to the European Parliament, the Council and the European Economic and Social Committee.

2. The evaluations shall establish whether additional rules, including regarding the list of core platform services laid down in point 2 of Article 2, the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 and their enforcement, may be required to ensure that digital markets across the Union are contestable and fair. Following the evaluations, the Commission shall take appropriate measures, which may include legislative proposals.

3. Member States shall provide any relevant information they have that the Commission may require for the purposes of drawing up the report referred to in paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Requires the Commission to periodically evaluate the Regulation and consider whether additional rules or legislative proposals are needed to ensure contestable and fair digital markets.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text is a general welcome of the Commission's initiative and a broad framing of gatekeeper platforms as "fundamental rights gatekeepers," without expressing any specific advocacy position on the review/evaluation mechanism established in Article 38. There is no directional stance on periodic reviews, their frequency, scope, or follow-up measures that could be evaluated against this provision.

article_title: Exemption for overriding reasons of public interest
article_text:
Article 9 — Exemption for overriding reasons of public interest
1. The Commission may, acting on a reasoned request by a gatekeeper or on its own initiative, by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), exempt it, in whole or in part, from a specific obligation laid down in Articles 5 and 6 in relation to an individual core platform service identified pursuant to Article 3(7), where such exemption is justified on the grounds set out in paragraph 2 of this Article. The Commission shall adopt the exemption decision at the latest 3 months after receiving a complete reasoned request.

2. An exemption pursuant to paragraph 1 may only be granted on grounds of:
(a)public morality;
(b)public health;
(c)public security.

3. The Commission may, acting on a reasoned request by a gatekeeper or on its own initiative, provisionally suspend the application of the relevant obligation to one or more individual core platform services already prior to the decision pursuant to paragraph 1.
In assessing the request, the Commission shall take into account, in particular, the impact of the compliance with the specific obligation on the grounds in paragraph 2 as well as the effects on the gatekeeper concerned and on third parties. The suspension may be made subject to conditions and obligations to be defined by the Commission in order to ensure a fair balance between the goals pursued by the grounds in paragraph 2 and the objectives of this Regulation. Such a request may be made and granted at any time pending the assessment of the Commission pursuant to paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing the Commission to exempt gatekeepers from specific obligations under Articles 5 and 6 on grounds of public morality, public health, or public security.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text expresses a general concern that large online platforms act as "fundamental rights gatekeepers" affecting privacy, data protection, and freedom of expression, but does not take any specific advocacy position on exemption mechanisms or the grounds for exempting gatekeepers from obligations. The text is a broad framing statement welcoming the Commission's initiative without directional advocacy on this specific provision.

article_text:
Article 23 — Commitments
1. If during proceedings under Articles 16 or 25 the gatekeeper concerned offers commitments for the relevant core platform services to ensure compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6, the Commission may by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) make those commitments binding on that gatekeeper and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

2. The Commission may, upon request or on its own initiative, reopen by decision the relevant proceedings, where:
(a)there has been a material change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)the gatekeeper concerned acts contrary to its commitments;
(c)the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the parties.

3. Should the Commission consider that the commitments submitted by the gatekeeper concerned cannot ensure effective compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6, it shall explain the reasons for not making those commitments binding in the decision concluding the relevant proceedings.
provision_effect: Establishes a commitment mechanism whereby gatekeepers may offer binding commitments to ensure compliance with obligations, and allows the Commission to reopen proceedings if circumstances change or commitments are breached.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text expresses a general welcome for the Commission's regulatory initiative and argues that large platforms act as "fundamental rights gatekeepers" affecting privacy, data protection, and freedom of expression — but it takes no specific stance on the commitment mechanism established in Article 23. There is no directional advocacy position regarding whether or how gatekeepers should be able to offer binding commitments to avoid further proceedings.

article_title: Review of the status of gatekeepers
article_text:
Article 4 — Review of the status of gatekeepers
1. The Commission may upon request or its own initiative reconsider, amend or repeal at any moment a decision adopted pursuant to Article 3 for one of the following reasons:
(a)there has been a substantial change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the undertakings.

2. The Commission shall regularly, and at least every 2 years, review whether the designated gatekeepers continue to satisfy the requirements laid down in Article 3(1), or whether new providers of core platform services satisfy those requirements. The regular review shall also examine whether the list of affected core platform services of the gatekeeper needs to be adjusted.
Where the Commission, on the basis of that review pursuant to the first subparagraph, finds that the facts on which the designation of the providers of core platform services as gatekeepers was based, have changed, it shall adopt a corresponding decision.

3. The Commission shall publish and update the list of gatekeepers and the list of the core platform services for which they need to comply with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 on an on-going basis.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to review, amend, or repeal gatekeeper designations, and requires regular biennial reviews of whether designated gatekeepers continue to meet the criteria in Article 3.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The organisation's text expresses general support for the Commission's initiative to regulate large online platforms as gatekeepers and broadens the framing to include fundamental rights concerns, but it does not take any specific stance on the review or re-designation mechanism established in Article 4. The position contains no directional advocacy regarding how or when gatekeeper status should be reviewed, amended, or repealed.

source_text: L’objectif est d’assurer un bon fonctionnement de la concurrence tout en favorisant le développement de plateformes numériques notamment européennes et l’innovation. La question d’une éventuelle réglementation / régulation qui serait applicable aux « grandes plateformes - gatekeepers » pose en préalable la question de leur définition. Elle conditionne à la fois l’efficacité des mesures qui seraient le cas échéant adoptées et le risque d’effets de bord négatifs qui pénaliseraient les entreprises européennes et traditionnelles dès lors qu’elles sont toutes engagées dans le développement du numérique et cherchent à créer et développer des plateformes. C’est dans ce cadre ainsi posé que le MEDEF présente ci-dessous, ses observations sur chacune des options présentées par la Commission européenne. 1. Option 1 : Révision du cadre horizontal défini dans le Règlement européen 2019/1150 – Platform-to-Business Regulation Comme l’indique la Commission européenne, le Règlement (UE) 2019/1150 constitue une première étape pour promouvoir l’équité et la transparence pour les entreprises utilisatrices de services d’intermédiation. Il poursuit un objectif différent de celui visé par la présente Étude d’impact qui vise à appréhender spécifiquement les questions de pouvoir de marché que détiennent les « grandes plateformes », qui leur permet de jouer un rôle dans l’accès à certains marchés (« gatekeeper »). Par voie de conséquence, le périmètre du Règlement est plus large (article premier – 2
context_before: Il est également nécessaire, en parallèle, d’assurer des conditions de concurrence équitables en prenant en compte des subventions étrangères accordées à des entreprises tierces également dans le cadre des échanges de services et du numérique. Le Livre blanc de la Commission européenne à ce sujet permettrait un contrôle au moins ex post des subventions destinées aux services. Ces outils devront être corrélés. La convergence des règles, à commencer au niveau européen, est clé dans le secteur du numérique, les services fournis étant sans frontières. Sur le fond, le MEDEF partage l’analyse de la Commission européenne sur la présentation du contexte et l’analyse des problèmes que posent les « grandes plateformes-gatekeepers » qui, par leurs caractéristiques et leur comportement, ont un impact sur le fonctionnement de la concurrence. Direction Juridique / Pôle Économie 30 juin 2020 5
context_after: Par voie de conséquence, le périmètre du Règlement est plus large (article premier – 2. du Règlement). Le MEDEF est donc d’avis que cet outil n’est pas adapté pour répondre aux objectifs poursuivis. Sa révision alourdirait la charge des plateformes numériques dont la plupart ne posent pas de problèmes de concurrence. Elle serait de nature à créer des effets contraires aux bénéfices escomptés d’une nouvelle réglementation qui devrait cibler les « grandes plateformes – gatekeepers », préalablement définies, pour ne pas pénaliser l’ensemble de l’économie. De plus, il est précisé que la révision s’inscrirait dans le cadre de l’article 18 § 2 du Règlement (au plus tard le 13 janvier 2022), qui paraît décalée par rapport à la nécessité d’agir vite. Le MEDEF n’est donc pas favorable à cette option. Direction Juridique / Pôle Économie 30 juin 2020 6
provision_effect: Establishes definitions for key terms used in the regulation, including 'gatekeeper', 'core platform services', and related concepts.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: MEDEF explicitly raises the question of how 'gatekeepers' and large platforms should be defined, warning that the definition conditions both the effectiveness of measures and the risk of negative side effects on European companies. The provision directly addresses this concern by establishing the definitions of 'gatekeeper' and 'core platform services'. However, MEDEF does not specify what the definition should or should not include, making it genuinely unclear whether the provision's specific definitional choices satisfy, contradict, or ignore MEDEF's concerns.

article_text:
Article 1 — Subject-matter and scope
1. This Regulation lays down harmonised rules ensuring contestable and fair markets in the digital sector across the Union where gatekeepers are present.

2. This Regulation shall apply to core platform services provided or offered by gatekeepers to business users established in the Union or end users established or located in the Union, irrespective of the place of establishment or residence of the gatekeepers and irrespective of the law otherwise applicable to the provision of service.

3. This Regulation shall not apply to markets:
(a)related to electronic communications networks as defined in point (1) of Article 2 of Directive (EU) 2018/1972 of the European Parliament and of the Council 37 ;
(b)related to electronic communications services as defined in point (4) of Article 2 of Directive (EU) 2018/1972 other than those related to interpersonal communication services as defined in point (4)(b) of Article 2 of that Directive.

4. With regard to interpersonal communication services this Regulation is without prejudice to the powers and tasks granted to the national regulatory and other competent authorities by virtue of Article 61 of Directive (EU) 2018/1972.

5. Member States shall not impose on gatekeepers further obligations by way of laws, regulations or administrative action for the purpose of ensuring contestable and fair markets. This is without prejudice to rules pursuing other legitimate public interests, in compliance with Union law. In particular, nothing in this Regulation precludes Member States from imposing obligations, which are compatible with Union law, on undertakings, including providers of core platform services where these obligations are unrelated to the relevant undertakings having a status of gatekeeper within the meaning of this Regulation in order to protect consumers or to fight against acts of unfair competition.

6. This Regulation is without prejudice to the application of Articles 101 and 102 TFEU. It is also without prejudice to the application of: national rules prohibiting anticompetitive agreements, decisions by associations of undertakings, concerted practices and abuses of dominant positions; national competition rules prohibiting other forms of unilateral conduct insofar as they are applied to undertakings other than gatekeepers or amount to imposing additional obligations on gatekeepers; Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 38 and national rules concerning merger control; Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 and Regulation (EU) …./.. of the European Parliament and of the Council 39 .

7. National authorities shall not take decisions which would run counter to a decision adopted by the Commission under this Regulation. The Commission and Member States shall work in close cooperation and coordination in their enforcement actions.
provision_effect: Establishes the subject matter and scope of the Digital Markets Act, applying harmonised rules to gatekeepers providing core platform services, while preempting additional Member State obligations and preserving existing competition law frameworks.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: MEDEF raises a specific concern about the definition of "gatekeepers" as a prerequisite condition for effective regulation, warning that an overly broad definition could negatively affect European companies. Article 1 establishes the scope of the regulation (applying to gatekeepers of core platform services) but does not itself define who qualifies as a gatekeeper — that definition appears elsewhere. Additionally, MEDEF's text references the P2B Regulation (2019/1150) and its broader perimeter, which Article 1(6) explicitly preserves, suggesting partial alignment; however, it is genuinely unclear whether the scope as drawn in Article 1 satisfies or contradicts MEDEF's concern about avoiding negative side-effects on European companies through an overly wide definition.
