context_before: L’objectif est d’assurer un bon fonctionnement de la concurrence tout en favorisant le développement de plateformes numériques notamment européennes et l’innovation. La question d’une éventuelle réglementation / régulation qui serait applicable aux « grandes plateformes - gatekeepers » pose en préalable la question de leur définition. Elle conditionne à la fois l’efficacité des mesures qui seraient le cas échéant adoptées et le risque d’effets de bord négatifs qui pénaliseraient les entreprises européennes et traditionnelles dès lors qu’elles sont toutes engagées dans le développement du numérique et cherchent à créer et développer des plateformes. C’est dans ce cadre ainsi posé que le MEDEF présente ci-dessous, ses observations sur chacune des options présentées par la Commission européenne. 1. Option 1 : Révision du cadre horizontal défini dans le Règlement européen 2019/1150 – Platform-to-Business Regulation Comme l’indique la Commission européenne, le Règlement (UE) 2019/1150 constitue une première étape pour promouvoir l’équité et la transparence pour les entreprises utilisatrices de services d’intermédiation. context_after: 2. article_title: Review of the status of gatekeepers
article_text:
Article 4 — Review of the status of gatekeepers
1. The Commission may upon request or its own initiative reconsider, amend or repeal at any moment a decision adopted pursuant to Article 3 for one of the following reasons:
(a)there has been a substantial change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the undertakings.

2. The Commission shall regularly, and at least every 2 years, review whether the designated gatekeepers continue to satisfy the requirements laid down in Article 3(1), or whether new providers of core platform services satisfy those requirements. The regular review shall also examine whether the list of affected core platform services of the gatekeeper needs to be adjusted.
Where the Commission, on the basis of that review pursuant to the first subparagraph, finds that the facts on which the designation of the providers of core platform services as gatekeepers was based, have changed, it shall adopt a corresponding decision.

3. The Commission shall publish and update the list of gatekeepers and the list of the core platform services for which they need to comply with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 on an on-going basis.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to review, amend, or repeal gatekeeper designations, including a mandatory review at least every 2 years, and requires publication of an updated list of gatekeepers and their core platform services.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: MEDEF explicitly advocates that regulation should target only pre-defined "grandes plateformes – gatekeepers" and criticises broader approaches that would burden platforms not posing competition problems. Article 4's review mechanism directly serves this goal by ensuring gatekeeper designations are regularly reassessed and can be repealed when facts change, thereby limiting the regulation's scope to those who genuinely qualify. MEDEF also criticises a revision timeline tied to a fixed date (January 2022) as misaligned with the need to act quickly, and Article 4's ongoing and at-least-biennial review process provides a more dynamic, continuous reassessment — consistent with MEDEF's directional preference for a targeted, regularly updated designation framework.

En particulier, la notion de « gatekeeper » doit être suffisamment et strictement définie pour couvrir les acteurs les plus structurants précisément identifiés, mais ne doit pas s’appliquer aux plateformes innovantes qui se trouveraient soumises à des contraintes disproportionnées et incompatibles avec le rôle que veut leur faire jouer l’Union européenne dans la mise en œuvre de la stratégie numérique. Deux options sont envisagées, complémentaires ou non : 3a. Prohibition de certaines pratiques commerciales déloyales mises en œuvre par les « grandes plateformes – gatekeepers » (« blacklisted » practices) Seraient définies, lorsqu’elles sont mises en œuvre par des « grandes plateformes – gatekeepers » des pratiques interdites (clause d’auto- préférence par exemple) applicables quel que soit le secteur dans lequel opère la plateforme, ainsi que des règles spécifiques concernant des problèmes associés à certains acteurs (systèmes d’exploitation, transparence algorithmique ou problèmes liés aux services de publicité en ligne). Le MEDEF est d’avis qu’une réglementation de ce type pourrait être utile et permettrait en effet d’appréhender certaines pratiques problématiques per se. context_after: 3b. Possibilité de remèdes sur mesure appliqués aux « grandes plateformes – gatekeepers »au cas par cas, lorsqu’ils sont nécessaires et justifiés Cette option devra traiter les plateformes les plus structurantes précisément identifiées afin de limiter les effets de bord non souhaités d’une nouvelle réglementation. Sous ces conditions, le MEDEF estime que cette proposition permettra d’appréhender les problématiques soulevées sous réserve que sa mise en œuvre assure une cohérence de la doctrine concurrence et évite toute contradiction entre des règles ex ante et ex post dont l’origine demeure le caractère incontournable de ces plateformes. Cette option nécessite donc d’être approfondie. Le MEDEF souligne également que la question des plateformes est multi- factorielle et qu’une réflexion supplémentaire s’impose, pour régler d’autres questions, d’ordre fiscal notamment. CONCLUSION ET PERSPECTIVES Le MEDEF poursuit avec ses adhérents sa réflexion sur le sujet plus large, « Concurrence et numérique », dont l’impact des plateformes sur le fonctionnement du marché est une composante majeure

label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: MEDEF explicitly advocates that the 'gatekeeper' notion must be sufficiently and strictly defined to cover only the most structurally significant actors, without applying to innovative platforms. The provision does define 'gatekeeper' and 'core platform services', which is directly relevant to MEDEF's concern. However, it is genuinely unclear whether the definitions as drafted are narrow enough to satisfy MEDEF's call for strict scoping — the provision lists a broad range of core platform services (including advertising, cloud, OS, etc.) which could be seen as either appropriately targeted or overly broad, making the direction of alignment ambiguous.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3432318 - 30/06/2020 30 June 2020 Microsoft’s Response to the Inception Impact Assessment - Digital Services Act package: ex ante regulatory instrument of very large online platforms acting as gatekeepers Microsoft appreciates the opportunity to comment on the European Commission’s Inception Impact Assessment regarding the development of an appropriate regulatory framework to address certain practices of online platforms acting as “Gatekeepers.” We support the Commission’s objective to ensure that markets impacted by Gatekeepers remain fair and contestable and to improve regulatory oversight over such platforms. As digital technologies increasingly enable commerce to shift online, the power of Gatekeepers to determine who wins and who loses grows. Defining the guardrails that govern such platforms is important to ensure that digital markets enable innovation and opportunity and, as we struggle with the impacts of COVID-19, enable and empower an inclusive economic recovery. More specifically, Microsoft supports Option 3, which allows for immediate action against problematic practices (unlike Option 2) with measures targeted at Gatekeepers, rather than all online intermediaries (like Option 1)
context_after: Furthermore, we believe a set of “blacklisted” practices (Option 3(a)) would be superior to a case-by-case remedies approach (Option 3(b)), which would blur the distinction between legislation and law enforcement and create a risk of duplication and confusion between the new ex ante regulatory instrument, competition enforcement and the proposed New Competition Tool. To succeed, we believe that the new ex ante regulation must be focused. To that end, it should clearly define the specific business models and behavioural and market characteristics that will lead an online platform to be identified as a Gatekeeper. It must also clearly define the obligations or prohibited practices, taking account of relevant differences in the sectors to which such obligations or prohibitions would apply. This will help to avoid overly broad inclusion and enforcement and unnecessarily deterring platforms, including Gatekeepers, from investing in new products and services and business practices that are not problematic. Additionally, it will be important that the regulation is capable of adapting and changing over time to keep pace with innovation in technologies and business practices in digital markets
article_text:
Article 8 — Suspension
1. The Commission may, on a reasoned request by the gatekeeper, exceptionally suspend, in whole or in part, a specific obligation laid down in Articles 5 and 6 for a core platform service by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), where the gatekeeper demonstrates that compliance with that specific obligation would endanger, due to exceptional circumstances beyond the control of the gatekeeper, the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union, and only to the extent necessary to address such threat to its viability. The Commission shall aim to adopt the suspension decision without delay and at the latest 3 months following receipt of a complete reasoned request.

2. Where the suspension is granted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall review its suspension decision every year. Following such a review the Commission shall either lift the suspension or decide that the conditions of paragraph 1 continue to be met.

3. The Commission may, acting on a reasoned request by a gatekeeper, provisionally suspend the application of the relevant obligation to one or more individual core platform services already prior to the decision pursuant to paragraph 1.
In assessing the request, the Commission shall take into account, in particular, the impact of the compliance with the specific obligation on the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union as well as on third parties. The suspension may be made subject to conditions and obligations to be defined by the Commission in order to ensure a fair balance between these interests and the objectives of this Regulation. Such a request may be made and granted at any time pending the assessment of the Commission pursuant to paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing gatekeepers to request suspension of specific obligations under Articles 5 and 6 where compliance would endanger their economic viability due to exceptional circumstances beyond their control.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Microsoft's submission expresses general support for the Commission's initiative and endorses Option 3 (targeted ex ante regulation of gatekeepers), but does not take any specific position on suspension mechanisms or viability-based exemptions from obligations. The org text contains no directional stance on whether such a suspension mechanism should or should not exist, making it impossible to evaluate alignment or opposition.

article_text:
Article 22 — Interim measures
1. In case of urgency due to the risk of serious and irreparable damage for business users or end users of gatekeepers, the Commission may, by decision adopt in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), order interim measures against a gatekeeper on the basis of a prima facie finding of an infringement of Articles 5 or 6.

2. A decision pursuant to paragraph 1 may only be adopted in the context of proceedings opened in view of the possible adoption of a decision of non-compliance pursuant to Article 25(1). This decision shall apply for a specified period of time and may be renewed in so far this is necessary and appropriate.
provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to order interim measures against a gatekeeper on the basis of a prima facie finding of infringement, in cases of urgency due to risk of serious and irreparable damage.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Microsoft explicitly supports "Option 3, which allows for immediate action against problematic practices," which directly corresponds to the interim measures mechanism in Article 22 that enables the Commission to act swiftly against gatekeepers. The provision delivers the kind of targeted, immediate enforcement action Microsoft advocated for, as opposed to slower or broader alternatives.

article_text:
Article 10 — Updating obligations for gatekeepers
1. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 34 to update the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 where, based on a market investigation pursuant to Article 17, it has identified the need for new obligations addressing practices that limit the contestability of core platform services or are unfair in the same way as the practices addressed by the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6.

1. A practice within the meaning of paragraph 1 shall be considered to be unfair or limit the contestability of core platform services where:
(a)there is an imbalance of rights and obligations on business users and the gatekeeper is obtaining an advantage from business users that is disproportionate to the service provided by the gatekeeper to business users; or
(b)the contestability of markets is weakened as a consequence of such a practice engaged in by gatekeepers.
provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts to update gatekeeper obligations in Articles 5 and 6 based on market investigations identifying new unfair or contestability-limiting practices.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Microsoft explicitly advocated that the regulation must be "capable of adapting and changing over time to keep pace with innovation in technologies and business practices in digital markets," and Article 10 establishes a concrete mechanism for updating gatekeeper obligations over time via delegated acts. Additionally, Microsoft favoured clearly defined "blacklisted" practices (rather than case-by-case remedies), which aligns with the provision's approach of updating a defined set of obligations in Articles 5 and 6 through a structured, criteria-based process rather than ad hoc enforcement.

article_title: Market investigation into new services and new practices
article_text:
Article 17 — Market investigation into new services and new practices
1. The Commission may conduct a market investigation with the purpose of examining whether one or more services within the digital sector should be added to the list of core platform services or to detect types of practices that may limit the contestability of core platform services or may be unfair and which are not effectively addressed by this Regulation. It shall issue a public report at the latest within 24 months from the opening of the market investigation.
Where appropriate, that report shall:
(a)be accompanied by a proposal to amend this Regulation in order to include additional services within the digital sector in the list of core platform services laid down in point 2 of Article 2;
(b)be accompanied by a delegated act amending Articles 5 or 6 as provided for in Article 10.
provision_effect: Establishes a market investigation mechanism allowing the Commission to examine whether new services should be added to core platform services or detect new unfair/anti-contestability practices, with a report due within 24 months and potential follow-up legislative or delegated acts.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Microsoft explicitly advocates for appropriate procedural safeguards in investigations, including separation of investigative and remedial phases, rights of defence, and guidelines to determine the type of process to follow (e.g., legislative proposal or imposition of remedies). Article 17 establishes a structured market investigation process with a distinct investigative phase (culminating in a public report) before any remedial action (legislative proposal or delegated act), which directly reflects Microsoft's ask for separation of investigative and remedial phases and a process-driven approach to determining outcomes.

article_text:
Article 15 — Market investigation for designating gatekeepers
1. The Commission may conduct a market investigation for the purpose of examining whether a provider of core platform services should be designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(6), or in order to identify core platform services for a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(7). It shall endeavour to conclude its investigation by adopting a decision in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) within twelve months from the opening of the market investigation.

2. In the course of a market investigation pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall endeavour to communicate its preliminary findings to the provider of core platform services concerned within six months from the opening of the investigation. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain whether it considers, on a provisional basis, that the provider of core platform services should be designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(6).

3. Where the provider of core platform services satisfies the thresholds set out in Article 3(2), but has presented significantly substantiated arguments in accordance with Article 3(4), the Commission shall endeavour to conclude the market investigation within five months from the opening of the market investigation by a decision pursuant to paragraph 1. In that case the Commission shall endeavour to communicate its preliminary findings pursuant to paragraph 2 to the provider of core platform services within three months from the opening of the investigation.

4. When the Commission pursuant to Article 3(6) designates as a gatekeeper a provider of core platform services that does not yet enjoy an entrenched and durable position in its operations, but it is foreseeable that it will enjoy such a position in the near future, it shall declare applicable to that gatekeeper only obligations laid down in Article 5(b) and Article 6(1) points (e), (f), (h) and (i) as specified in the designation decision. The Commission shall only declare applicable those obligations that are appropriate and necessary to prevent that the gatekeeper concerned achieves by unfair means an entrenched and durable position in its operations. The Commission shall review such a designation in accordance with the procedure laid down in Article 4.

article_text:
Article 8 — Suspension
1. The Commission may, on a reasoned request by the gatekeeper, exceptionally suspend, in whole or in part, a specific obligation laid down in Articles 5 and 6 for a core platform service by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), where the gatekeeper demonstrates that compliance with that specific obligation would endanger, due to exceptional circumstances beyond the control of the gatekeeper, the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union, and only to the extent necessary to address such threat to its viability. The Commission shall aim to adopt the suspension decision without delay and at the latest 3 months following receipt of a complete reasoned request.

2. Where the suspension is granted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall review its suspension decision every year. Following such a review the Commission shall either lift the suspension or decide that the conditions of paragraph 1 continue to be met.

3. The Commission may, acting on a reasoned request by a gatekeeper, provisionally suspend the application of the relevant obligation to one or more individual core platform services already prior to the decision pursuant to paragraph 1.
In assessing the request, the Commission shall take into account, in particular, the impact of the compliance with the specific obligation on the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union as well as on third parties. The suspension may be made subject to conditions and obligations to be defined by the Commission in order to ensure a fair balance between these interests and the objectives of this Regulation. Such a request may be made and granted at any time pending the assessment of the Commission pursuant to paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing gatekeepers to request suspension of specific obligations under Articles 5 and 6 where compliance would endanger their economic viability due to exceptional circumstances beyond their control.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Vodafone's position focuses on the design of the gatekeeper designation framework, the flexibility of regulatory approaches, and criteria for defining gatekeepers — it does not express any specific stance on suspension mechanisms for gatekeeper obligations. The org text contains no substantive advocacy position related to the suspension procedure established in Article 8.

article_text:
Article 16 — Market investigation into systematic non-compliance
1. Where the market investigation shows that a gatekeeper has systematically infringed the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 and has further strengthened or extended its gatekeeper position in relation to the characteristics under Article 3(1), the Commission may by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) impose on such gatekeeper any behavioural or structural remedies which are proportionate to the infringement committed and necessary to ensure compliance with this Regulation. The Commission shall conclude its investigation by adopting a decision within twelve months from the opening of the market investigation.

2. The Commission may only impose structural remedies pursuant to paragraph 1 either where there is no equally effective behavioural remedy or where any equally effective behavioural remedy would be more burdensome for the gatekeeper concerned than the structural remedy.

3. A gatekeeper shall be deemed to have engaged in a systematic non-compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6, where the Commission has issued at least three non-compliance or fining decisions pursuant to Articles 25 and 26 respectively against a gatekeeper in relation to any of its core platform services within a period of five years prior to the adoption of the decision opening a market investigation in view of the possible adoption of a decision pursuant to this Article.

4. A gatekeeper shall be deemed to have further strengthened or extended its gatekeeper position in relation to the characteristics under Article 3(1), where its impact on the internal market has further increased, its importance as a gateway for business users to reach end users has further increased or the gatekeeper enjoys a further entrenched and durable position in its operations.

5. The Commission shall communicate its objections to the gatekeeper concerned within six months from the opening of the investigation. In its objections, the Commission shall explain whether it preliminarily considers that the conditions of paragraph 1 are met and which remedy or remedies it preliminarily considers necessary and proportionate.

6. The Commission may at any time during the market investigation extend its duration where the extension is justified on objective grounds and proportionate. The extension may apply to the deadline by which the Commission has to issue its objections, or to the deadline for adoption of the final decision. The total duration of any extension or extensions pursuant to this paragraph shall not exceed six months.The Commission may consider commitments pursuant to Article 23 and make them binding in its decision.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism for the Commission to impose behavioural or structural remedies on gatekeepers found to have systematically infringed obligations under Articles 5 and 6, defining the conditions and procedures for such market investigations.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Vodafone's text expresses general support for a flexible ex ante regulatory framework targeting gatekeepers and raises procedural questions (e.g., who designates gatekeepers, how remedies are set and enforced), but does not take a specific directional stance on the mechanism of systematic non-compliance investigations or the imposition of structural/behavioural remedies. The comments are largely exploratory questions and broad endorsements of a framework concept, without a specific advocacy position that can be evaluated against this provision's concrete action.

article_text:
Article 23 — Commitments
1. If during proceedings under Articles 16 or 25 the gatekeeper concerned offers commitments for the relevant core platform services to ensure compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6, the Commission may by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) make those commitments binding on that gatekeeper and declare that there are no further grounds for action.

2. The Commission may, upon request or on its own initiative, reopen by decision the relevant proceedings, where:
(a)there has been a material change in any of the facts on which the decision was based;
(b)the gatekeeper concerned acts contrary to its commitments;
(c)the decision was based on incomplete, incorrect or misleading information provided by the parties.

3. Should the Commission consider that the commitments submitted by the gatekeeper concerned cannot ensure effective compliance with the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6, it shall explain the reasons for not making those commitments binding in the decision concluding the relevant proceedings.
provision_effect: Establishes a commitments mechanism whereby gatekeepers can offer binding commitments to ensure compliance with obligations, and sets conditions under which the Commission may reopen proceedings or decline to make commitments binding.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3433132 - 30/06/2020 Preliminary views on the European Commission’s Impact Assessments regarding the Digital Services Act and New Competition Tool 1. INTRODUCTION 1. On June 2, 2020, the European Commission (“Commission”) launched public consultations regarding a proposed Digital Services Act (“DSA”) and a proposed New Competition Tool (“NCT”). The Commission has invited comments on the Inception Impact Assessments that accompany both public consultations. Oracle views many of the issues raised by both consultations to concern the relationship between both instruments, and therefore submits its comments on both in this document. 2. Oracle agrees with the Commission’s findings that certain companies have become unavoidable trading partners in the digital economy and that structural competition problems exist that EU competition law currently cannot (sufficiently) address. Oracle believes the Commission needs additional powers to ensure that fair competition in the EU Single Market can prevail to the benefit of consumers, and therefore supports the Commission’s proposals. 2. THE PROBLEMS IDENTIFIED BY THE COMMISSION 3. First, the DSA Impact Assessment notes that a small number of large online platforms are increasingly insulated from competition and can easily leverage their advantages from one area of their activity to expand into adjacent markets, for example because of the vast amounts of data they collect
context_after: Second, the NCT Impact Assessment notes that there exist structural competition problems that EU competition rules cannot tackle or cannot address in the most effective manner, which can generally be grouped into two categories depending on whether harm is about to affect or has already affected the market: (i) structural risks for competition or (ii) structural lack of competition. 3. ORACLE’S VIEWS 4. Oracle agrees with the Commission’s assessment that certain large online platforms take up an ever more prominent role in our digital economy and can wield enormous market power vis-à-vis consumers or smaller economic operators in an unequal bargaining position. Oracle believes the proposed DSA could be an effective tool to curb the market power of these companies, especially when designed to outright prohibit certain unfair practices they are prone to engage in. In addition, Oracle believes adoption of the NCT would grant the Commission much-needed powers to investigate competition problems whenever they arise, to the benefit of fair competition in the Single Market and, ultimately, European consumers. i. Interplay between EU competition law and DSA and/or NCT 5. Competition law is a versatile tool. It has over the years proven itself able to deal with various types of anticompetitive behaviour – even when the conduct at issue was novel or took place 1
article_title: Right to be heard and access to the file
article_text:
Article 30 — Right to be heard and access to the file
1. Before adopting a decision pursuant to Article 7, Article 8(1), Article 9(1), Articles 15, 16, 22, 23, 25 and 26 and Article 27(2), the Commission shall give the gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned the opportunity of being heard on:
(a)preliminary findings of the Commission, including any matter to which the Commission has taken objections;
(b)measures that the Commission may intend to take in view of the preliminary findings pursuant to point (a) of this paragraph.

2. Gatekeepers, undertakings and associations of undertakings concerned may submit their observations to the Commission’s preliminary findings within a time limit which shall be fixed by the Commission in its preliminary findings and which may not be less than 14 days.

3. The Commission shall base its decisions only on objections on which gatekeepers, undertakings and associations of undertakings concerned have been able to comment.

4. The rights of defence of the gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned shall be fully respected in any proceedings. The gatekeeper or undertaking or association of undertakings concerned shall be entitled to have access to the Commission's file under the terms of a negotiated disclosure, subject to the legitimate interest of undertakings in the protection of their business secrets. The right of access to the file shall not extend to confidential information and internal documents of the Commission or the authorities of the Member States. In particular, the right of access shall not extend to correspondence between the Commission and the authorities of the Member States. Nothing in this paragraph shall prevent the Commission from disclosing and using information necessary to prove an infringement.
provision_effect: Establishes the right of gatekeepers and undertakings to be heard, submit observations, access the Commission's file, and have their rights of defence fully respected before the Commission adopts decisions under the DMA.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Oracle's submission expresses general support for the Commission's proposals to address structural competition problems and endorses the need for additional powers, but contains no specific advocacy position regarding procedural rights such as the right to be heard, access to the file, or rights of defence. The org text is a general endorsement without any directional stance on the procedural safeguards established by Article 30.

Oracle considers the Commission should define a set of criteria that ensure the DSA is focused on companies with exceptional market power such as Google, without unduly extending the scope of application to, e.g., companies that may have large operations but that by no means can wield excessive market power vis-à-vis consumers or smaller economic operators in an unequal bargaining position. 9. In that context, it is not clear to Oracle whether the designation as “gatekeeper platform” will be based on a set of objective criteria that can be applied mechanically or whether the Commission will designate companies as “gatekeeper platforms” on a case-by-case basis, exercising discretion, weighing several criteria and reaching a conclusion based on all relevant circumstances. Oracle believes the Commission should define a clear set of objective criteria that must be weighed against each other in individual cases, in whatever permutation of the DSA is ultimately adopted. 10

article_text:
Article 15 — Market investigation for designating gatekeepers
1. The Commission may conduct a market investigation for the purpose of examining whether a provider of core platform services should be designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(6), or in order to identify core platform services for a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(7). It shall endeavour to conclude its investigation by adopting a decision in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) within twelve months from the opening of the market investigation.

2. In the course of a market investigation pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall endeavour to communicate its preliminary findings to the provider of core platform services concerned within six months from the opening of the investigation. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain whether it considers, on a provisional basis, that the provider of core platform services should be designated as a gatekeeper pursuant to Article 3(6).

3. Where the provider of core platform services satisfies the thresholds set out in Article 3(2), but has presented significantly substantiated arguments in accordance with Article 3(4), the Commission shall endeavour to conclude the market investigation within five months from the opening of the market investigation by a decision pursuant to paragraph 1. In that case the Commission shall endeavour to communicate its preliminary findings pursuant to paragraph 2 to the provider of core platform services within three months from the opening of the investigation.

4. When the Commission pursuant to Article 3(6) designates as a gatekeeper a provider of core platform services that does not yet enjoy an entrenched and durable position in its operations, but it is foreseeable that it will enjoy such a position in the near future, it shall declare applicable to that gatekeeper only obligations laid down in Article 5(b) and Article 6(1) points (e), (f), (h) and (i) as specified in the designation decision. The Commission shall only declare applicable those obligations that are appropriate and necessary to prevent that the gatekeeper concerned achieves by unfair means an entrenched and durable position in its operations. The Commission shall review such a designation in accordance with the procedure laid down in Article 4.
provision_effect: Establishes a market investigation procedure for the Commission to designate providers of core platform services as gatekeepers, including timelines, preliminary findings, and conditions for applying obligations to providers not yet entrenched.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: Oracle advocates for a clear set of objective criteria for gatekeeper designation, rather than pure case-by-case discretion, and wants the scope limited to companies with exceptional market power. Article 15 establishes a market investigation procedure that involves Commission discretion in designating gatekeepers under Article 3(6), which could be seen as the case-by-case approach Oracle was cautious about — but the provision also operates within the framework of thresholds and criteria set elsewhere (Article 3(2)), making it genuinely unclear whether this satisfies or contradicts Oracle's position on objective criteria versus discretionary designation.

Oracle believes the blacklist ex ante regulatory framework will be more straightforward to enforce than, and hence is preferable to, a regulatory framework on the basis of which tailor- made remedies can be adopted following a prior assessment of the conduct of the gatekeeper platform in question. The Commission would in any event be able to impose tailor-made remedies under the NCT. 16. One of the stated aims of the NCT is to increase speed of intervention, especially in fast- moving digital markets where harm to competition can quickly become irreversible. While Oracle believes the need for speed could partially be achieved by more aggressive antitrust enforcement and by making use of tools already available to the Commission, such as interim measures, we agree that the NCT could potentially help the Commission intervene more rapidly, especially in the broadest proposed permutation

Given that the Commission would only be able to intervene in circumstances where it finds structural competition problems 3
context_after: However, Oracle invites the Commission to consider in detail how it will achieve speed while ensuring procedural fairness, given that a market investigation of an entire sector is not inherently faster than a standard competition investigation. In standard competition investigations significant time is dedicated to ensuring parties' procedural rights are observed, e.g., in relation to contestations of confidentiality designations, which potentially would be equally relevant to market investigations. In addition, the Commission needs to be clear about the standard of proof for demonstrating potential harmful effects to competition it foresees using in the context of the NCT, and how this standard would be different, if at all, from that in a traditional antitrust investigation. The Commission also needs to clearly lay out what the benefit of the NCT would be over the traditional sector inquiries it can already conduct. 4. CONCLUSION 18. Oracle supports the Commission’s efforts to ensure competition in the Single Market can work properly to the benefit of consumers. Oracle believes the proposed DSA and NCT could be appropriate tools to help the Commission achieve that goal. 19. Oracle remains available to discuss its preliminary views with the Commission should it wish to do so. 4

Enforcement of the blacklist ex ante regulatory framework at EU level would ensure sufficient funding for active enforcement, would prevent forum shopping and would ensure much- needed consistency in the application of the rules. b) Enforcement of the NCT 17. One of the stated aims of the NCT is to increase speed of intervention, especially in fast- moving digital markets where harm to competition can quickly become irreversible. While Oracle believes the need for speed could partially be achieved by more aggressive antitrust enforcement and by making use of tools already available to the Commission, such as interim measures, we agree that the NCT could potentially help the Commission intervene more rapidly, especially in the broadest proposed permutation

Given that the Commission would only be able to intervene in circumstances where it finds structural competition problems 3
context_after: However, Oracle invites the Commission to consider in detail how it will achieve speed while ensuring procedural fairness, given that a market investigation of an entire sector is not inherently faster than a standard competition investigation. In standard competition investigations significant time is dedicated to ensuring parties' procedural rights are observed, e.g., in relation to contestations of confidentiality designations, which potentially would be equally relevant to market investigations. In addition, the Commission needs to be clear about the standard of proof for demonstrating potential harmful effects to competition it foresees using in the context of the NCT, and how this standard would be different, if at all, from that in a traditional antitrust investigation. The Commission also needs to clearly lay out what the benefit of the NCT would be over the traditional sector inquiries it can already conduct. 4. CONCLUSION 18. Oracle supports the Commission’s efforts to ensure competition in the Single Market can work properly to the benefit of consumers. Oracle believes the proposed DSA and NCT could be appropriate tools to help the Commission achieve that goal. 19. Oracle remains available to discuss its preliminary views with the Commission should it wish to do so. 4
article_text:
Article 25 — Non-compliance
1. The Commission shall adopt a non-compliance decision in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4) where it finds that a gatekeeper does not comply with one or more of the following:
(a)any of the obligations laid down in Articles 5 or 6;
(b)measures specified in a decision adopted pursuant to Article 7(2);
(c)measures ordered pursuant to Article 16(1);
(d)interim measures ordered pursuant to Article 22; or
(e)commitments made legally binding pursuant to Article 23.

2. Before adopting the decision pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall communicate its preliminary findings to the gatekeeper concerned. In the preliminary findings, the Commission shall explain the measures it considers to take or it considers that the gatekeeper should take in order to effectively address the preliminary findings.

3. In the non-compliance decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall order the gatekeeper to cease and desist with the non-compliance within an appropriate deadline and to provide explanations on how it plans to comply with the decision.

4. The gatekeeper shall provide the Commission with the description of the measures it took to ensure compliance with the decision adopted pursuant to paragraph 1.

5. Where the Commission finds that the conditions of paragraph 1 are not met, it shall close the investigation by a decision.
provision_effect: Establishes the procedure for the Commission to adopt non-compliance decisions against gatekeepers who fail to meet obligations, including ordering them to cease and desist and provide compliance plans.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: Oracle explicitly advocates for a blacklist ex ante regulatory framework that is more straightforward to enforce, with EU-level enforcement ensuring consistency and speed of intervention. The provision establishes a concrete enforcement mechanism (non-compliance decisions, cease-and-desist orders) against gatekeepers, which aligns with Oracle's advocacy for robust, active EU-level enforcement of the regulatory framework for gatekeeper platforms.

article_text:
Article 22 — Interim measures
1. In case of urgency due to the risk of serious and irreparable damage for business users or end users of gatekeepers, the Commission may, by decision adopt in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), order interim measures against a gatekeeper on the basis of a prima facie finding of an infringement of Articles 5 or 6.

2. A decision pursuant to paragraph 1 may only be adopted in the context of proceedings opened in view of the possible adoption of a decision of non-compliance pursuant to Article 25(1). This decision shall apply for a specified period of time and may be renewed in so far this is necessary and appropriate.
provision_effect: Establishes the Commission's power to order interim measures against a gatekeeper on the basis of a prima facie finding of infringement, in cases of urgency due to risk of serious and irreparable damage.

The provision directly establishes exactly this mechanism — Commission-ordered interim measures — which Oracle advocated for as a means of rapid intervention to prevent irreversible harm.

article_text:
Article 19 — Requests for information
1. The Commission may by simple request or by decision require information from undertakings and associations of undertakings to provide all necessary information, including for the purpose of monitoring, implementing and enforcing the rules laid down in this Regulation. The Commission may also request access to data bases and algorithms of undertakings and request explanations on those by a simple request or by a decision.

2. The Commission may request information from undertakings and associations of undertakings pursuant to paragraph 1 also prior to opening a market investigation pursuant to Article 14 or proceedings pursuant to Article 18.

3. When sending a simple request for information to an undertaking or association of undertakings, the Commission shall state the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and fix the time-limit within which the information is to be provided, and the penalties provided for in Article 26 for supplying incomplete, incorrect or misleading information or explanations.

4. Where the Commission requires undertakings and associations of undertakings to supply information by decision, it shall state the purpose of the request, specify what information is required and fix the time-limit within which it is to be provided. Where the Commission requires undertakings to provide access to its data-bases and algorithms, it shall state the legal basis and the purpose of the request, and fix the time-limit within which it is to be provided. It shall also indicate the penalties provided for in Article 26 and indicate or impose the periodic penalty payments provided for in Article 27. It shall further indicate the right to have the decision reviewed by the Court of Justice.

5. The undertakings or associations of undertakings or their representatives shall supply the information requested on behalf of the undertaking or the association of undertakings concerned. Lawyers duly authorised to act may supply the information on behalf of their clients. The latter shall remain fully responsible if the information supplied is incomplete, incorrect or misleading.

6. At the request of the Commission, the governments and authorities of the Member States shall provide the Commission with all necessary information to carry out the duties assigned to it by this Regulation.
provision_effect: Establishes the Commission's power to request information, access to databases and algorithms from undertakings, either by simple request or by decision, including prior to opening a market investigation or proceedings.
label: NOISE
reasoning: Oracle's position focuses on procedural fairness, speed of investigations, standard of proof, and the added value of the NCT over traditional sector inquiries — it does not express any specific stance on the Commission's information-gathering powers or requests for access to databases and algorithms. The org text contains only general support for the Commission's competition goals and broad procedural concerns, with no directional advocacy position on the subject matter of Article 19.

source_text: Ref. Ares(2020)3433936 - 30/06/2020 EBF_041853 30 June 2020 EBF response to European Commission Inception Impact Assessment on an ex- ante regulatory instrument for large online platforms with significant network effects acting as gate-keepers in the European Union’s internal market 1. Introduction The European Banking Federation (EBF) welcomes the European Commission’s initiative to address the issues with regard to large online platforms with significant network effects acting as gate-keepers and welcomes the opportunity to respond to the published Inception Impact Assessment (IIA) on a possible ex-ante regulatory instrument. We support the Commission’s assessment of both the positive aspects and the challenges presented by online platforms. There is a wide recognition that online platforms have been instrumental to the growth of digital markets and have helped spur both efficiency gains and innovation across the wider economy, including supporting SME engagement in the digital economy. At the same time, the nature of online platforms and the consequences of their growth and position in the modern digital economy has resulted in increasing concerns over how to ensure that digital markets remain conducive to fair competition and continue to generate the same opportunities and incentives for innovation in the future
context_after: We agree with the Commission that several platforms have developed particularly crucial roles, with the potential for their actions and decisions to have outsized effects on the European digital economy and to set “the parameters for future innovations, consumer choice and competition” across the Single Market. We would therefore like to highlight in this response two particularly pressing issues, related to the role of data held by large online platforms and of technical infrastructure operated or controlled by large online platforms in the digital economy. These will also be expanded upon together with other points in our response to the Commission’s consultation on the Digital Services Act Package. 2. Data gatekeepers There is widespread consensus that data has become a strategic asset in the digital economy. Access to and re-use of data is considered a crucial step towards a competitive EU data economy which will benefit both consumers and EU firms. This is recognized by the European Commission’s Data Strategy which notes that “ Data is the lifeblood of economic development: it is the basis for many new products and services, driving productivity and resource efficiency gains across all sectors of the economy, allowing for European Banking Federation aisbl Brussels / Avenue des Arts 56, 1000 Brussels, Belgium / +32 2 508 3711 / info@ebf.eu Frankfurt / Weißfrauenstraße 12-16, 60311 Frankfurt, Germany EU Transparency Register / ID number: 4722660838-23 www.ebf.eu
article_text:
Article 8 — Suspension
1. The Commission may, on a reasoned request by the gatekeeper, exceptionally suspend, in whole or in part, a specific obligation laid down in Articles 5 and 6 for a core platform service by decision adopted in accordance with the advisory procedure referred to in Article 32(4), where the gatekeeper demonstrates that compliance with that specific obligation would endanger, due to exceptional circumstances beyond the control of the gatekeeper, the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union, and only to the extent necessary to address such threat to its viability. The Commission shall aim to adopt the suspension decision without delay and at the latest 3 months following receipt of a complete reasoned request.

2. Where the suspension is granted pursuant to paragraph 1, the Commission shall review its suspension decision every year. Following such a review the Commission shall either lift the suspension or decide that the conditions of paragraph 1 continue to be met.

3. The Commission may, acting on a reasoned request by a gatekeeper, provisionally suspend the application of the relevant obligation to one or more individual core platform services already prior to the decision pursuant to paragraph 1.
In assessing the request, the Commission shall take into account, in particular, the impact of the compliance with the specific obligation on the economic viability of the operation of the gatekeeper in the Union as well as on third parties. The suspension may be made subject to conditions and obligations to be defined by the Commission in order to ensure a fair balance between these interests and the objectives of this Regulation. Such a request may be made and granted at any time pending the assessment of the Commission pursuant to paragraph 1.
provision_effect: Establishes a mechanism allowing the Commission to suspend, on a gatekeeper's request, specific obligations under Articles 5 and 6 where compliance would endanger the gatekeeper's economic viability due to exceptional circumstances beyond its control.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The EBF's text expresses only a general welcome of the Commission's initiative to regulate large online platforms and acknowledges both their benefits and concerns about fair competition, without taking any specific stance on suspension mechanisms or the conditions under which gatekeepers should be relieved of obligations. There is no directional advocacy position that can be evaluated against this provision.

article_text:
Article 12 — Obligation to inform about concentrations
1. A gatekeeper shall inform the Commission of any intended concentration within the meaning of Article 3 of Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 involving another provider of core platform services or of any other services provided in the digital sector irrespective of whether it is notifiable to a Union competition authority under Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 or to a competent national competition authority under national merger rules.
A gatekeeper shall inform the Commission of such a concentration prior to its implementation and following the conclusion of the agreement, the announcement of the public bid, or the acquisition of a controlling interest.

2. The notification pursuant to paragraph 1 shall at least describe for the acquisition targets their EEA and worldwide annual turnover, for any relevant core platform services their respective EEA annual turnover, their number of yearly active business users and the number of monthly active end users, as well as the rationale of the intended concentration.

3. If, following any concentration as provided in paragraph 1, additional core platform services individually satisfy the thresholds in point (b) of Article 3(2), the gatekeeper concerned shall inform the Commission thereof within three months from the implementation of the concentration and provide the Commission with the information referred to in Article 3(2).
provision_effect: Requires gatekeepers to inform the Commission of any intended concentration involving providers of core platform services or digital sector services, regardless of whether it meets standard merger notification thresholds.
label: NOISE
reasoning: The EBF's text contains only a general welcome of the Commission's initiative and broad support for addressing gatekeeper issues, with no specific advocacy position on merger notification obligations or concentration reporting requirements. There is no directional stance that can be evaluated against this specific provision.

context_after: more personalised products and services and enabling better policy making and upgrading government services.” The competitiveness of firms thus increasingly depends on timely access to relevant data, yet the current data sharing framework puts certain players at a disadvantage (e.g. PSD2 regime) while others act as data gatekeepers – as in the case of large online platforms. For example, BigTech firms have started to leverage control over the customer interaction and extensive data to enter new sectors, including financial services. The Inception Impact Assessment recognizes this trend: “An ability of a small number of large online platforms to comparatively easily enter adjacent markets, since they benefit from the use of data gathered from one area of their activity to improve or develop new services in these adjacent markets, increases a risk of these adjacent markets also tipping in favour of these platforms to the detriment of innovation and consumer choice.” Addressing concentrated data collection through new measures therefore presents an opportunity to improve both traditional competition “for the market”, for example between similar platform services, as well as to ensure that data remains in the consumers control and is used to support competition and innovation in adjacent markets, by increasing the availability of a key input for innovative data-driven services and thereby helping to limit anti-competitive leveraging or envelopment strategies 3

The position is a general endorsement of the Commission's concern about platform power and a framing of data issues, without advocating for or against any specific element of the designation mechanism.

source_text:
1. Ensure Interoperability The platform economy is characterized by network effects, economies of scale and feedback loops that have propelled some intermediaries into gatekeeper positions, arbitrating access to services, goods and information. To strengthen users’ self-determination and encourage innovation, we propose ex ante interoperability obligations for platforms with significant market power. Such intermediaries should be mandated to make technical interfaces available through which competitors can interoperate with the incumbent’s main functionalities. This should include the obligation to ensure users’ right to data portability. To make interoperability work, users should have the right to delegate certain services to third parties they trust. That way, interoperability obligations could also be an innovative approach to issues regarding content moderation, targeted advertisement or harassment online.

2. No commercial use of data Any data shared for the purpose of interoperability should not be available for commercial use. Data made available in the context of interoperability should only be used for maintaining interoperability, safeguarding users’ privacy, or ensuring data security. This should also positively incentivize competitors with innovative, responsible, and privacy-protective business models.
context_before:
The Electronic Frontier Foundation (EFF) is among the leading non-profit organizations defending civil liberties in the digital world. We welcome the opportunity to provide feedback to the European Commission and to highlight what matters most: the E-Commerce Directive should be apt to deal with the challenges brought by a digital space that is monopolized by a few platforms and where alternative platform models and voices of persons in a vulnerable position are often marginalized. Our suggestions for the upcoming Digital Services Acts thus center on strengthening users’ self-determination, maintaining the core pillars of the Directive that have fostered freedom of expression, and establishing procedural safeguards. 

For the purpose of this Impact Assessment, we support the introduction of ex-ante regulation for online platforms with significant network effects, and we would like to elaborate on why there is a need to introduce interoperability obligations.
context_after:
3. Privacy It is crucial to empower users to take control of how, when, why, and with whom their data is being shared. This means that key principles underpinning the GDPR —such as data minimization, privacy by design, and privacy by default—must be respected. This should also include easy-to-use interfaces through which users can give their explicit consent regarding any use of their data (as well as revoke that consent at any time).

4. Security  Users’ data and communications should not only be kept private, but also safe. Interoperability measures should always center on users’ security and should never be construed as a reason that prevents platforms from taking efforts to keep users safe. However, if intermediaries do have to suspend interoperability to fix security issues, they should not exploit such situations to break interoperability but rather communicate transparently, resolve the problem, and reinstate interoperability interfaces within a reasonable and clearly defined time-frame.
article_title: Obligations for gatekeepers susceptible of being further specified
provision_effect: Establishes a set of obligations for gatekeepers including data portability for end users, interoperability requirements for third-party software and ancillary services, and restrictions on commercial use of business user data not publicly available.
label: ALIGNED
reasoning: The EFF explicitly advocates for ex ante interoperability obligations, data portability rights, and restrictions on commercial use of data shared for interoperability purposes. The provision directly delivers on all three: Article 6(f) mandates interoperability with operating system/hardware features, Article 6(h) requires effective data portability tools for end users, and Article 6(a) prohibits gatekeepers from using business users' non-public data for their own competitive purposes — closely mirroring EFF's call that interoperability data should not be available for commercial use.

provision_effect: Article 5(1) establishes a set of specific behavioural obligations for gatekeepers, including restrictions on combining personal data without consent, allowing business users to operate outside gatekeeper platforms, and prohibiting forced bundling of services.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The EFF specifically advocates for mandatory interoperability obligations and data portability rights for platforms with significant market power, which is a related but distinct ask from what Article 5 delivers. While Article 5(a) touches on data combination restrictions (partially resonant with EFF's "no commercial use of data" position) and Article 5(e)-(f) address some bundling/forced sign-in issues, the provision does not establish the affirmative interoperability mandates or technical interface obligations that EFF explicitly calls for. There is a genuine thematic connection, but it is unclear whether the provision satisfies, contradicts, or simply falls short of EFF's specific advocacy for ex ante interoperability obligations.

Our suggestions for the upcoming Digital Services Acts thus center on strengthening users’ self-determination, maintaining the core pillars of the Directive that have fostered freedom of expression, and establishing procedural safeguards. 

For the purpose of this Impact Assessment, we support the introduction of ex-ante regulation for online platforms with significant network effects, and we would like to elaborate on why there is a need to introduce interoperability obligations.
context_after:
3. Privacy It is crucial to empower users to take control of how, when, why, and with whom their data is being shared. This means that key principles underpinning the GDPR —such as data minimization, privacy by design, and privacy by default—must be respected. This should also include easy-to-use interfaces through which users can give their explicit consent regarding any use of their data (as well as revoke that consent at any time).

4. Security  Users’ data and communications should not only be kept private, but also safe. Interoperability measures should always center on users’ security and should never be construed as a reason that prevents platforms from taking efforts to keep users safe. However, if intermediaries do have to suspend interoperability to fix security issues, they should not exploit such situations to break interoperability but rather communicate transparently, resolve the problem, and reinstate interoperability interfaces within a reasonable and clearly defined time-frame.
article_text:
Article 10 — Updating obligations for gatekeepers
1. The Commission is empowered to adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 34 to update the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6 where, based on a market investigation pursuant to Article 17, it has identified the need for new obligations addressing practices that limit the contestability of core platform services or are unfair in the same way as the practices addressed by the obligations laid down in Articles 5 and 6.

1. A practice within the meaning of paragraph 1 shall be considered to be unfair or limit the contestability of core platform services where:
(a)there is an imbalance of rights and obligations on business users and the gatekeeper is obtaining an advantage from business users that is disproportionate to the service provided by the gatekeeper to business users; or
(b)the contestability of markets is weakened as a consequence of such a practice engaged in by gatekeepers.
provision_effect: Empowers the Commission to adopt delegated acts updating gatekeeper obligations (Articles 5 and 6) based on market investigations identifying new unfair or contestability-limiting practices.
label: UNDETECTABLE
reasoning: The EFF specifically advocates for ex ante interoperability obligations and data portability rights for platforms with significant market power — a concrete directional ask. Article 10 creates a mechanism to update gatekeeper obligations to address unfair or contestability-limiting practices, which could in principle encompass interoperability requirements, but the provision itself does not establish or reference interoperability obligations. It is genuinely unclear whether this updating mechanism would be used to deliver what EFF advocates for, making the direction of alignment ambiguous rather than clear.

source_text:
1. Ensure Interoperability The platform economy is characterized by network effects, economies of scale and feedback loops that have propelled some intermediaries into gatekeeper positions, arbitrating access to services, goods and information. To strengthen users’ self-determination and encourage innovation, we propose ex ante interoperability obligations for platforms with significant market power. Such intermediaries should be mandated to make technical interfaces available through which competitors can interoperate with the incumbent’s main functionalities. This should include the obligation to ensure users’ right to data portability. To make interoperability work, users should have the right to delegate certain services to third parties they trust. That way, interoperability obligations could also be an innovative approach to issues regarding content moderation, targeted advertisement or harassment online.

2. No commercial use of data Any data shared for the purpose of interoperability should not be available for commercial use. Data made available in the context of interoperability should only be used for maintaining interoperability, safeguarding users’ privacy, or ensuring data security. This should also positively incentivize competitors with innovative, responsible, and privacy-protective business models.
